base_rate_description: Across knowledge-worker job changes tracked in longitudinal surveys, ~60% of movers report they were glad they switched at the 2-year mark. Conditional on a >15% comp increase AND meaningful scope change, satisfaction rises to ~75%. Lateral comp moves trend ~50/50.
common_regret_patterns: Movers who optimized purely on comp without scope change report the highest regret at 12 months. Those who moved primarily to escape a specific manager report high satisfaction if the new manager is a known quantity, middling if unknown.

base_rate_description: About 20% of new US businesses fail in year 1; ~50% by year 5; ~65% by year 10 (BLS BED data). Conditional on prior domain experience and >6 months runway, failure rates at 5 years drop closer to 35-40%. Self-reported founder satisfaction, separate from financial success, is higher than comparable employee cohorts even among those whose businesses fail.
common_regret_patterns: Founders with young children and no co-founder report the highest burnout-driven regret. Under-capitalized founders who took on personal debt (vs. equity) report the most acute financial regret even when businesses survive.

base_rate_description: Outcomes vary wildly by field. Professional degrees (medicine, law, engineering masters) have high completion rates (>85%) and typically positive lifetime earnings premiums. Humanities PhDs have attrition rates near 50% and mixed labor-market outcomes. Average MBA ROI is positive but concentrated at top-tier programs.
common_regret_patterns: Debt-funded humanities and arts PhDs correlate with the highest regret. Technical masters taken between 2-5 years of work experience (not straight from undergrad) report the strongest satisfaction-to-cost ratio.

base_rate_description: Small-sample surveys of people who took 3-12 month career breaks report high satisfaction (~70-80%) with the break itself, but mixed re-entry outcomes: ~30% accept lower-titled or lower-paid roles on return. The discount is smaller when the break is framed and documented (travel, caregiving, sabbatical) than when it reads as an unexplained gap.
common_regret_patterns: Breaks under 3 months rarely produce the restorative effect people hope for. Breaks longer than 12 months without a structured activity (project, travel, education) correlate with higher re-entry anxiety and depression.

base_rate_description: Population-level US divorce rate is ~35-40% over a lifetime, but stratifies heavily: religious practice, age at marriage, cohabitation history, and SES each move the estimate by 10-20 points.
common_regret_patterns: Couples who married young (<23) without shared faith or financial stability report the highest divorce and dissatisfaction rates. Religious couples who share denomination and attend services weekly report the lowest.

base_rate_description: Longitudinal surveys of divorcees find that roughly 55-65% report the divorce was the right call 5+ years later, but ~30% report regret, particularly among those who initiated without attempting serious counseling. For unmarried long-term relationships, regret rates are less well-studied but trend similar. Children under 10 in the home shift the regret rate upward.
common_regret_patterns: Initiators who didn't attempt couples therapy report the highest retrospective regret. When abuse or addiction is present, regret rates drop sharply — the ending is rarely questioned.

base_rate_description: Parent satisfaction surveys consistently show 90%+ of parents report they would make the choice again. Self-reported day-to-day happiness briefly dips in the first two years (especially for mothers), then recovers. Long-term life-meaning measures are substantially higher for parents than non-parents who wanted children but did not have them.
common_regret_patterns: The rare retrospective regret clusters among solo parents with minimal support and those whose partners were ambivalent. Prospective regret ('should have waited') is far more common than 'should have had a child' regret among people who did.

base_rate_description: Retrospective satisfaction for each additional child remains very high (>85%) through child #3, with a modest decline thereafter. Siblings reliably provide long-term social and emotional benefit to each other into adulthood. Financial strain is the largest mediator of reduced parent satisfaction at higher parity.
common_regret_patterns: Families who had an additional child primarily from external pressure (in-laws, cultural expectations) without full partner alignment report the highest strain. Financial regret is common where housing is inadequate but rarely extends to the child themselves.

base_rate_description: ~55% of workers who relocate for a job report satisfaction with the move at 2 years, but ~70% when a partner was also excited about the destination, and ~35% when the partner was reluctant.
common_regret_patterns: Reluctant partners are the single largest predictor of regret. People who moved away from aging parents report specific regret about missed milestones.

base_rate_description: Movers who relocate to be within an hour of extended family report high satisfaction (~70-80%) when the move is driven by positive pull (childcare help, aging-parent proximity) rather than negative push (financial necessity). Quality of the specific family relationships dominates outcome — moving near a supportive parent correlates with high satisfaction; moving near a high-conflict family does not.
common_regret_patterns: Couples who moved toward one partner's family without an honest conversation about expectations (how often to visit, caregiving load) report higher friction at 1-2 years. Career regret is most acute for the partner whose field is thin in the new city.

base_rate_description: Long-run US homeownership satisfaction is high (>80% would buy again). Financial outcomes depend heavily on purchase timing relative to local market cycles and length of ownership. Buying with a stay-horizon of <5 years significantly raises the risk of negative financial outcome after transaction costs. Buying at or near the top of a local cycle with aggressive leverage is the most consequential regret driver.
common_regret_patterns: Homes bought under time pressure (contingent on new-job start, or a 3-day offer window) correlate with higher regret. Stretching to the top of an approved mortgage correlates strongly with chronic financial stress regardless of home satisfaction.

base_rate_description: Behavioral-finance research on lottery winners and inheritance recipients finds that ~70% of windfalls are depleted within 5 years when received without a plan. Recipients who allocated the windfall within 30 days of receipt (paying debt, investing a defined percentage, setting aside a specific 'fun' allocation) report dramatically higher long-term financial satisfaction than those who left it unallocated in a checking account.
common_regret_patterns: Windfalls shared with extended family under emotional pressure, without written expectations, produce the deepest relational regret. Windfalls spent on lifestyle inflation (new car, boat, upgraded house) rather than one-time experiences are the least remembered positively.

base_rate_description: Experiential purchases (travel, lessons, events) produce more durable satisfaction than material purchases of similar cost in nearly every study measuring 6+ months out. For material goods, satisfaction is highest when the item is used daily and replaces a worse version of the same thing; it is lowest for aspirational purchases bought before the matching lifestyle has materialized.
common_regret_patterns: Aspirational purchases (high-end hobby gear for a hobby not yet established) have the highest regret rate. Purchases financed with revolving debt are almost never remembered positively after the debt is counted.

base_rate_description: Outcomes are so condition-specific that base rates from a generic 'medical decision' category are rarely useful. The consistent finding across fields is that patients who sought a second opinion from a different institution are more satisfied with their care decision, regardless of whether the second opinion changed the plan. Shared-decision-making research shows that patients who used a formal decision aid report lower decisional regret at 6-12 months.

sources.author: Dartmouth Center for Shared Decision Making
sources.title: Patient Decision Aids

common_regret_patterns: Decisions made under acute time pressure without a second opinion produce the most retrospective regret, even when the chosen intervention was clinically correct. The feeling of having been rushed dominates the memory.

subcategory: choosing a school for a child
base_rate_description: Cross-sectional research on school choice shows parents who actively chose their child's school (public-choice, charter, private, homeschool) report higher satisfaction than those who defaulted to assigned zoning, independent of the actual academic outcomes. Parental involvement and peer-group quality predict student outcomes more than which school type is selected.
common_regret_patterns: Private-school choices made primarily for status signaling (not fit with the child) correlate with higher switching in later grades. Homeschool choices made without a specific plan for socialization and high-school-level subjects correlate with later regret.

base_rate_description: Surveys of individual contributors promoted into people-management find roughly 50-60% are satisfied with the move at 2 years; satisfaction is markedly higher when the person enjoyed mentoring before the title and lower when they took it mainly for pay or status. A sizeable minority (~20-30%) attempt to return to an IC track within a few years, and report relief rather than failure when they do.
common_regret_patterns: ICs who took management purely as the only promotion path (no IC ladder) and missed the craft report the highest regret. Those who inherited a large or troubled team with no onboarding support burn out fastest.

base_rate_description: Cohabitation outcomes hinge on intent more than the act: couples who moved in together as a deliberate step toward a shared future report satisfaction comparable to engaged couples, while those who 'slid' into it for convenience or cost report higher rates of later conflict and breakup. Pre-existing agreement on marriage and children is the strongest moderator.
common_regret_patterns: Couples who moved in primarily to save on rent without discussing where the relationship is headed report the most friction. Unequal domestic-labor expectations surface within the first year and predict dissatisfaction.

base_rate_description: Expatriate satisfaction studies show a common U-curve: an initial honeymoon, a trough at 6-18 months (the hard part of language, bureaucracy, and isolation), then recovery for those who stay past two years. Roughly 25-40% of voluntary expats return earlier than planned, most citing family ties and difficulty building local community rather than the job itself. Outcomes are far better when a partner or family is genuinely on board.
common_regret_patterns: Moves made for a single job without a plan to build local roots, or over a reluctant partner, drive the highest early-return regret. Underestimating the 6-18 month trough leads people to quit just before adjustment would have arrived.

subcategory: quitting drinking, smoking, or a major habit
base_rate_description: Most people attempting to quit a major habit relapse on early attempts — smoking quit attempts succeed perhaps 5-10% unaided per try, much higher with support and medication — but cumulative success across repeated attempts is high, and the number of prior attempts predicts eventual success rather than failure. Retrospective satisfaction among those who sustained the change for a year is very high and rarely reversed.
stratification_variables: use of structured support or medication
common_regret_patterns: Going it alone with willpower only, with no replacement routine and unchanged social triggers, is the most common path to relapse. Treating a relapse as total failure (rather than data for the next attempt) is itself a strong predictor of giving up.

base_rate_description: Family caregiving decisions (in-home care, moving a parent in, or assisted living) involve a well-documented tradeoff: in-home and co-residence preserve relationship closeness but carry high caregiver burnout, while quality facility care reduces caregiver strain but carries guilt that fades over time as the parent's care needs are met. Caregiver satisfaction is highest when siblings share the load explicitly and when the parent's own wishes were discussed in advance.
common_regret_patterns: Solo caregivers who took on co-residence without sibling support or respite report the deepest burnout and resentment. Decisions made in crisis after a fall or hospitalization, with no prior family conversation, produce the most second-guessing regardless of which option was chosen.
